expanded_description: Congratulations! You have completed Unit 4: Chemical Bonding. This unit covered the fundamental forces that hold atoms together, from ionic and covalent bonds to intermolecular forces that determine physical properties. You are now ready to proceed to Unit 5.

core_concept.principle: Unit 4 Completion: You have mastered chemical bonding concepts including ionic bonding (electron transfer), covalent bonding (electron sharing), Lewis structures, electronegativity, bond polarity, molecular polarity, and intermolecular forces.
core_concept.explanation: Understanding chemical bonding is foundational for all future chemistry topics. The concepts you've learned in Unit 4—how atoms connect, how molecular structure affects properties, and how intermolecular forces determine physical behavior—will be essential for understanding chemical reactions, solutions, organic chemistry, and biochemistry in upcoming units.

image.alt: Summary diagram of Unit 4 concepts showing bonding continuum from ionic to covalent to intermolecular forces
image.caption: Unit 4 Summary: Bonding exists on a continuum from ionic (electron transfer) to covalent (electron sharing) to intermolecular forces (between molecules).

sections.introduction: Before moving to Unit 5, let's review the essential concepts from Unit 4 that will be foundational for future learning.

key_concept.definition: Unit 4 covered: (1) Ionic bonding—electron transfer creating ions held by electrostatic forces; (2) Covalent bonding—electron sharing between atoms; (3) Lewis structures—visual representation of valence electrons and bonding; (4) Electronegativity and bond polarity—unequal electron sharing creating partial charges; (5) Molecular polarity—combination of bond polarity and geometry; (6) Intermolecular forces—London dispersion, dipole-dipole, and hydrogen bonding.
key_concept.context: These concepts interconnect: electronegativity determines bond type, bond type affects molecular geometry, geometry affects molecular polarity, and polarity determines intermolecular forces which determine physical properties.

classifications.value: Ionic (ΔEN ≥ 1.7), Polar Covalent (0.4 ≤ ΔEN < 1.7), Nonpolar Covalent (ΔEN < 0.4)
classifications.behavior: Bond type determines compound properties: ionic compounds have high mp/bp and conduct when dissolved; covalent compounds have lower mp/bp and variable conductivity.

examples.explanation: Na transfers electron to Cl, forming Na⁺ and Cl⁻ held by electrostatic attraction. High mp (801°C), conducts when dissolved.
O and H share electrons unequally (O is δ⁻, H is δ⁺). Moderate mp (0°C), polar molecule with H-bonding.
C and H share electrons nearly equally. Low mp (-182°C), nonpolar molecule with only London forces.

classifications.value: Count valence electrons, arrange atoms, distribute electrons, form multiple bonds if needed
characteristics: Octet rule (8 e⁻ for most atoms)
Resonance when multiple valid structures exist
classifications.behavior: Lewis structures predict molecular geometry, bond types, and polarity; exceptions include incomplete octets (Be, B) and expanded octets (Period 3+).

examples.explanation: 16 valence e⁻, C central, O=C=O double bonds. Linear geometry, nonpolar molecule despite polar bonds.
8 valence e⁻ (5 from N + 4 from H - 1 for charge), N central with 4 H atoms. Tetrahedral, +1 charge on N.

classifications.value: London dispersion (all molecules) < Dipole-dipole (polar) < Hydrogen bonding (H-N/O/F)
characteristics: London: temporary dipoles from electron fluctuations
H-bonding: H bonded to N, O, or F
classifications.behavior: Stronger IMFs = higher boiling point, higher viscosity, higher surface tension, lower vapor pressure. IMFs determine solubility ('like dissolves like').

examples.explanation: H₂O (bp 100°C) has H-bonding; H₂S (bp -60°C) has only dipole-dipole. H-bonding creates 160°C difference despite similar mass.
I₂ (solid) has stronger London forces (more electrons) than F₂ (gas). Both nonpolar, but size affects London force strength.

sections.introduction: Unit 5 typically covers chemical reactions and stoichiometry in most chemistry curricula. However, the specific topics depend on your course sequence. Please provide your Unit 5 outline so I can create content matching your curriculum.

key_concept.definition: Common Unit 5 topics in chemistry include: (1) Chemical Reactions & Equations—types of reactions, balancing equations, predicting products; (2) Stoichiometry—mole ratios, limiting reactants, percent yield; (3) Solutions & Concentration—molarity, dilution, solution stoichiometry; (4) Gases—gas laws, ideal gas law, gas stoichiometry.
key_concept.context: These topics build on Unit 4 bonding knowledge: understanding bonds helps predict reaction types; molecular formulas from Lewis structures are needed for stoichiometry; intermolecular forces explain solution behavior.

principles.statement: Chemical reactions involve breaking bonds in reactants and forming new bonds in products; bonding knowledge from Unit 4 predicts reaction types and products.
principles.application: Ionic compounds undergo double replacement reactions; covalent compounds undergo combustion, synthesis, or decomposition; redox reactions involve electron transfer.
principles.example: NaCl(aq) + AgNO₃(aq) → NaNO₃(aq) + AgCl(s). Ionic compounds exchange ions; AgCl precipitates because it's insoluble (strong ionic lattice).
principles.analogy: Like a dance: partners (atoms) switch partners during the reaction, but everyone must have a partner at the end (balanced equation).

principles.statement: Stoichiometric calculations require correct molecular formulas, which you learned to determine from Lewis structures and bonding rules in Unit 4.
principles.application: Mole ratios from balanced equations enable calculation of reactant/product amounts; limiting reactant determines maximum product yield.
principles.example: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. From Unit 4, you know H₂ and O₂ are diatomic, H₂O has 2 H per O. Stoichiometry uses these ratios for calculations.
principles.analogy: Like a recipe: 2 cups flour + 1 cup sugar → 12 cookies. Stoichiometry tells you how much product from given ingredients.

principles.statement: Solution formation and solubility depend on intermolecular forces between solute and solvent; 'like dissolves like' from Unit 4 applies directly.
principles.application: Polar solutes dissolve in polar solvents (ion-dipole, H-bonding); nonpolar solutes dissolve in nonpolar solvents (London forces).
principles.example: NaCl dissolves in water (ion-dipole forces) but not in hexane. Oil dissolves in hexane (London forces) but not in water.
principles.analogy: Like social groups: similar people (polar/polar or nonpolar/nonpolar) mix well; different people don't mix.

worked_examples.title: Connecting Unit 4 to Unit 5: Reaction Prediction
worked_examples.problem: Using your Unit 4 bonding knowledge, predict the products and balance the equation for: Mg + O₂ → ? Explain the bond types in reactants and products.

step_by_step.calculation: Mg: metal (metallic bonding); O₂: nonpolar covalent molecule (O=O double bond)

step_by_step.explanation: Determine product formula from ion charges.
step_by_step.calculation: Mg forms Mg²⁺ (Group 2); O forms O²⁻ (Group 16); Product: MgO (charges balance: +2 + -2 = 0)
2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO (2 Mg on each side, 2 O on each side)

worked_examples.final_answer: 2Mg + O₂ → 2MgO. Reactants: Mg (metallic bonding), O₂ (nonpolar covalent). Product: MgO (ionic bonding). This demonstrates how Unit 4 bonding knowledge enables Unit 5 reaction prediction: metal + nonmetal → ionic compound, balanced using charge knowledge from Lewis structures.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Connecting Unit 4 bonding concepts to Unit 5 reaction prediction

sections.introduction: To continue creating content that matches your specific curriculum, please provide the Unit 5 section outline (similar to how you provided outlines for Units 2, 3, and 4).

key_concept.definition: Please share your Unit 5 sections list, such as: 5.1 Chemical Reactions, 5.2 Balancing Equations, 5.3 Reaction Types, 5.4 Stoichiometry, etc. This ensures content matches your course sequence and learning objectives.
key_concept.context: Once you provide the Unit 5 outline, I will create detailed JSON content following the same schema as Units 2-4, with expanded descriptions, core concepts, sections with principles/classifications/examples/worked problems, key terms, and summaries.

principles.statement: Unit 5 content will follow the same displayTextbookSection schema as Units 2-4, ensuring consistency across your textbook.
principles.application: Each section will include: expanded_description, core_concept (principle, explanation, image), sections array (with introductions, key_concepts, classifications, principles, components, worked_examples), key_terms, and summary.
principles.example: Just as Unit 4.7 (Electronegativity) had worked examples calculating ΔEN and predicting bond types, Unit 5 stoichiometry sections will have worked examples with step-by-step calculations.
principles.analogy: Like chapters in a book: same format throughout makes navigation and learning easier.

principles.statement: Unit 5 content will explicitly connect to Unit 4 concepts, reinforcing bonding knowledge while introducing new reaction and stoichiometry concepts.
principles.application: Stoichiometry problems will reference molecular formulas from Lewis structures; solution chemistry will reference intermolecular forces; reaction types will reference bond types.
principles.example: When calculating moles of H₂O produced, we'll reference that H₂O has 2 H atoms per molecule (from Unit 4 Lewis structure knowledge).
principles.analogy: Like building a house: Unit 4 laid the foundation (bonding); Unit 5 builds the structure (reactions) on that foundation.

worked_examples.title: Example Unit 5 Section Preview
worked_examples.problem: Here's a preview of how Unit 5 content will be structured once you provide the outline. This example shows Section 5.1: Introduction to Chemical Reactions.

step_by_step.explanation: Section will open with expanded_description and core_concept.
step_by_step.calculation: Core concept: Chemical reactions transform reactants into products by breaking and forming bonds

step_by_step.explanation: Sections will include classifications (reaction types), principles (conservation of mass), components (equation parts).
step_by_step.calculation: Reaction types: synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion

step_by_step.explanation: Worked examples will show step-by-step problem solving.
step_by_step.calculation: Example: Balance Fe + O₂ → Fe₂O₃ with step-by-step coefficients

step_by_step.explanation: Key terms and summary will reinforce learning.
step_by_step.calculation: Key terms: reactant, product, coefficient, subscript, balanced equation, etc.

worked_examples.final_answer: Unit 5 content will follow this structure for each section. Please provide your Unit 5 outline (section titles 5.1 through 5.10 or however many sections your unit has), and I will create complete, detailed JSON content matching this format with all the educational elements from Units 2-4.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Preview of Unit 5 content structure based on Units 2-4 schema

sections.introduction: Celebrate your completion of Unit 4! You have mastered essential chemistry concepts that will serve as the foundation for all future chemistry learning.

key_concept.definition: Unit 4 Learning Outcomes Achieved: (1) Distinguish ionic vs. covalent bonding; (2) Draw Lewis structures for atoms, ions, and molecules; (3) Predict bond polarity from electronegativity; (4) Determine molecular polarity from geometry; (5) Identify intermolecular forces; (6) Relate IMFs to physical properties.
key_concept.context: These skills are essential for: predicting chemical reactions (Unit 5), understanding solutions (Unit 5/6), studying organic chemistry (later units), and comprehending biochemistry (proteins, DNA, enzymes all depend on bonding and IMFs).

principles.statement: Unit 4 concepts apply across all chemistry subdisciplines: organic (covalent bonding patterns), biochemistry (H-bonding in DNA/proteins), materials (ionic vs. covalent solids), environmental (solubility and pollution).
principles.application: Drug design uses polarity for bioavailability; polymer science uses bonding for material properties; environmental chemistry uses solubility for pollutant behavior.
principles.example: DNA double helix: H-bonding between base pairs (Unit 4) enables replication (biology). Aspirin solubility: polar/nonpolar balance (Unit 4) enables absorption (pharmacology).
principles.analogy: Like learning alphabet before reading: Unit 4 bonding is the 'alphabet' of chemistry; Units 5+ are the 'words and sentences' you can now read.

summary: Unit 4: Chemical Bonding is complete! You have mastered: ionic bonding (electron transfer, crystal lattices, ionic compounds), covalent bonding (electron sharing, Lewis structures, single/double/triple bonds), electronegativity and bond polarity (ΔEN determines bond type), molecular polarity (bond polarity + geometry), and intermolecular forces (London dispersion, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonding). These concepts are foundational for Unit 5 (chemical reactions and stoichiometry) and all future chemistry learning. Bonding knowledge enables reaction prediction, molecular formula determination, and understanding of physical properties. To continue, please provide your Unit 5 section outline (e.g., 5.1 Chemical Reactions, 5.2 Balancing Equations, 5.3 Stoichiometry, etc.), and I will create detailed content following the same schema as Units 2-4. Unit 5 will build on Unit 4: reactions break/form bonds, stoichiometry uses molecular formulas from Lewis structures, solutions depend on intermolecular forces. Your bonding knowledge is the 'alphabet' of chemistry—now you're ready to read the 'words and sentences' of reactions and stoichiometry!
